reasoning:
The market is asking if federal employment (via FRED) will decline by more than 250,000 between January 2025 and January 2026. The initial market probability on July 24 was ~28%. Recent BLS jobs reports indicate federal employment drops of modest sizes: ~10k in Feb, 15k in March, 22k in May, and 12k in July. This net monthly reduction (~15,000 average) represents a combination of a hiring freeze and normal attrition not being replaced. In 6 months, this has yielded ~80,000 to 90,000 net jobs lost. 

A large 'Deferred Resignation Program' (DRP) will see an estimated 75,000 workers drop from the payrolls in October 2025. If the ~15k/month net reduction continues for the remaining 6 months of the year (+90k) and the 75k DRP reduction is realized, the total net reduction would be roughly 85k + 90k + 75k = 250,000. However, the trajectory has slowed slightly (12k drop in July compared to 22k in May), and there is resistance (courts reinstating 24,000 workers). A specialized tracking site (DailyFED) estimated total separations reaching 100,000 to 200,000, missing the 250,000 threshold. Given the legal pushback, recent slowing in the net reduction rate, and the fact that 250,000 requires near-perfect execution of the remaining planned cuts without any offsets, achieving a net drop of >250,000 appears slightly less likely than the late-July market prediction, leading to an updated probability of 21%.
